raw_consumer_statement:
In the first week of XX/XX/year>, I discovered XXXX fraudulent charges to my Citi XXXX credit card. Purchases were made on XX/XX/XXXX and XX/XX/year>, all from websites I had never before shopped, or even visited for that matter. When I discovered the charges, I immediately contacted Citi to report them and to request cancellation of the compromised card. Citi investigated the charges and issued credits for XXXX of them but said that the merchant ( levis.com ) provided documentation proving that the XXXX disputed charge was valid. The documentation provided does not in fact prove that the charge was valid. Citi stated that because the IP address from which the items were ordered was in XXXX and they had " delivery confirmation '', that was sufficient proof. I'm well aware that my personal information has been compromised in several data leaks, which is why my credit reports have been frozen for a long time and why I monitor all of my accounts closely. It doesn't surprise me that someone in XXXX got ahold of my credit card information and personal details. A fraudulent purchase made from a XXXX XXXX address proves only that the fraudster had my personal information and was in XXXX, or using a VPN that would indicate such. 

As for " delivery confirmation '', the items in question were delivered to an address in XXXX, SC, a city I'm entirely unfamiliar with and certainly not randomly sending $ XXXX dollars worth of apparel to. I have no connection whatsoever to the address that received the goods nor to anyone who resides there and I refuse to pay $ XXXX for merchandise I never ordered nor received. I need Citi to do the right thing and reverse this charge.
